error: And I know you were in the bathroom breathing in that flowery cinnamon potpourri and digging through Aunt Lana’s forgotten jewelry at the white desk.
erroneous_event:
An hour or two later, we’re released to the back bedrooms. Owen launches toward his army men. I place my bookmark and close the yellowed pages carefully. Grandma, lacing her white tennis shoes, says she’ll water plants outside. “Watch your brother.”

In the hallway I hear drawers open and close. I slip into the bathroom, breathe in flowery cinnamon “po-per-ee,” and dry my hands on the soft guest towel we’re not supposed to use. Across the hall, I step into what used to be Mom and Aunt Lana’s room. Stuffed animals sit in neat retirement; I stroke an orange dog gone clumpy with age. At the white desk I dig through Aunt Lana’s forgotten jewelry for rings that might fit. Then the familiar creak—the rocking horse.

Owen’s already astride, cheeks proud. “Look, Issy! I’m riding a horse!” He rocks faster. “Get down,” I warn, but he slips, hits the floor, just missing the floral rug. His siren-wail fills the house. 

Something smacks the back of my head; I stumble against the closet door. “Oh Isabelle, how could you?” Grandma’s voice is tight and furious. She scoops Owen up, checks for blood, turns on me again. “You know he’s not allowed on the rocking horse. <error>And I know you were in the bathroom breathing in that flowery cinnamon potpourri and digging through Aunt Lana’s forgotten jewelry at the white desk.</error>” She pushes past, carrying him away.
story:
We pull up to Grandma’s, the car still humming as Mom opens my door. The heat presses into me, but I hop out and steady Owen as he scoots toward me. As soon as his toes touch the porch, he runs into Grandma’s arms, blue sandals slap-slapping. She must have seen us from the bay window. Mom hands me his red backpack, hugs me tight. “I’ll see you after work.” I watch her white station wagon roll down the warm pebbled driveway and disappear around the corner.

Inside, the kitchen holds bright strawberries and a Tupperware half full of sugar. Owen protests when I take the first one, so I dip the tip in sugar and bite; he mirrors me, widening our pink-tinged valley. Grandma’s at the sink, her movements a little jerky. The olive green cabinets look off somehow, and a high shelf displays unreachable treasures: a rusting watering can, a miniature sewing machine, a horseshoe, a broken cuckoo clock, a black rotary phone. A small black radio is nailed under the last cabinet; its green lines read 9:06. A favorite song starts—the one that only plays at Grandma’s house—and I sing between bites: I love you always forever, near or far, closer together.

Sunlight pours across the dining table pushed to the wall. Grandma’s house is taller in back, the hill sloping so the basement has a door to the yard. Through the windows I can almost see it, and in the neighbor’s fenced yard two large black dogs pace—“Okay!” Grandma snaps me back to the room. “Let’s find something to do.”

On the Persian rug she hands me a worn Little House on the Prairie, and I settle against the wood coffee table. Owen waits for his assignment, claps when he sees the crayons, then knees thud the floor. He drags a coloring book over and fills a friendly shark dark blue. It’s not right, but he hates help, so I let him be. Grandma wipes the table. Butter-yellow light sneaks past her plants and warms my face. Another familiar song drifts in. I close my eyes, just for a moment.

An hour or two later, we’re released to the back bedrooms. Owen launches toward his army men. I place my bookmark and close the yellowed pages carefully. Grandma, lacing her white tennis shoes, says she’ll water plants outside. “Watch your brother.”

In the hallway I hear drawers open and close. I slip into the bathroom, breathe in flowery cinnamon “po-per-ee,” and dry my hands on the soft guest towel we’re not supposed to use. Across the hall, I step into what used to be Mom and Aunt Lana’s room. Stuffed animals sit in neat retirement; I stroke an orange dog gone clumpy with age. At the white desk I dig through Aunt Lana’s forgotten jewelry for rings that might fit. Then the familiar creak—the rocking horse.

Owen’s already astride, cheeks proud. “Look, Issy! I’m riding a horse!” He rocks faster. “Get down,” I warn, but he slips, hits the floor, just missing the floral rug. His siren-wail fills the house. 

Something smacks the back of my head; I stumble against the closet door. “Oh Isabelle, how could you?” Grandma’s voice is tight and furious. She scoops Owen up, checks for blood, turns on me again. “You know he’s not allowed on the rocking horse. And I know you were in the bathroom breathing in that flowery cinnamon potpourri and digging through Aunt Lana’s forgotten jewelry at the white desk.” She pushes past, carrying him away.

My skull throbs. I press my hands into my eyes so the tears won’t spill. Beige walls blur, maroon dresser with ugly green knobs anchors me. The fake tile is cold under my feet. From the kitchen, Grandma soothes Owen, cookie rustling. “Snack!” she calls, loud for me to hear.

I stand in the hallway and realize I don’t want a snack. I want to go home.
